owasp_top10: A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
mitre_attack: T1070.002 - Indicator Removal: Clear Linux or Mac System Logs
T1562.002 - Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging
T1070.003 - Indicator Removal: Clear Command History
T1222.002 - File and Directory Permissions Modification: Linux and Mac File and Directory Permissions Modification
cwe: CWE-732 - Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource

threat.title: Log Tampering via Writable Log Files in Linux/Docker Environments
threat.description: When application or system log files have overly permissive write permissions (e.g., 666, 644 with incorrect ownership), attackers who gain initial access can modify, delete, or inject false entries into logs to hide their activities. This eliminates audit trails, prevents forensic investigation, and allows attackers to operate undetected for extended periods. In containerized environments, this risk is amplified when containers run as root or mount host log directories with write access.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker gains initial access to system via compromised application or stolen SSH credentials
2. Discovers application logs at /var/log/app/application.log with world-writable permissions (666)
3. Uses sed or echo to delete or modify incriminating log entries showing their initial compromise
4. Injects false log entries with backdated timestamps to create plausible deniability
5. For Docker containers, mounts host log directory with write access and modifies host audit logs
6. Deletes authentication logs (/var/log/auth.log) or uses log rotation to push evidence out of retention period
7. Operates undetected for months while exfiltrating data, with no forensic trail remaining
prerequisites: Log files have insecure permissions (world-writable or writable by application user)
Container running as root or with elevated privileges
No log forwarding to remote SIEM or centralized logging system
Lack of file integrity monitoring on log files
No immutable storage or write-once-read-many (WORM) logging configured

cve_references.description: Docker Engine in debug mode adds secrets to debug logs, enabling information exposure and potential log tampering vectors
Docker Desktop records environment variables in application logs, exposing sensitive credentials that could be tampered with to hide evidence
runC vulnerability allowing attackers to gain writable access to /proc and container filesystems, enabling log file modification

real_world_breaches.incident: Ivanti Connect Secure VPN Breach by UNC5221
real_world_breaches.impact: Over 2,100 Ivanti VPN appliances backdoored; CISA issued emergency directive requiring 48-hour disconnection. Chinese espionage group exfiltrated sensitive files and deleted logs to cover tracks using SPAWNSLOTH log tampering utility.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Attackers exploited zero-day vulnerabilities (CVE-2023-46805, CVE-2024-21887) and systematically deleted logs using custom malware to evade detection and hinder forensic investigation

real_world_breaches.impact: 18,000+ organizations including US federal agencies compromised. Attackers maintained access for months undetected by manipulating build logs and disabling security logging.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Attackers disabled logging during DLL injection, re-enabled it afterward, and scrubbed logs/alerts before detection. Build logs were modified to hide backdoor code insertion.

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Container escape via writable /var/log mount allowing host log modification and evidence destruction

security_research.title: Anti-Forensics Techniques: Log Tampering and Deletion
security_research.key_finding: Log tampering is one of the five most common anti-forensics techniques used by attackers to evade detection and hinder incident response

security_research.title: Why Log Tampering is So Crucial to Cybercrime
security_research.key_finding: If logs are not read-only, attackers can modify them to hide from automated alarms and disguise attack methods, severely hindering defensive capabilities

mitigations.title: Implement Restrictive Log File Permissions (chmod 640)
mitigations.description: Configure log files with secure permissions (640 or 600) ensuring only root and specific logging groups can write. Application processes should write through syslog rather than directly to files. In Docker containers, run as non-root users and use read-only root filesystems.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Secure log file permissions on Linux hosts

# Set restrictive permissions on application logs
chmod 640 /var/log/app/*.log
chown root:adm /var/log/app/*.log

# Set secure permissions on system logs
chmod 640 /var/log/auth.log /var/log/syslog /var/log/kern.log
chown syslog:adm /var/log/auth.log /var/log/syslog

# Configure rsyslog to create files with secure permissions
cat >> /etc/rsyslog.conf << 'EOF'
# Set default file creation mask
$FileCreateMode 0640
$DirCreateMode 0755
$Umask 0022
EOF

systemctl restart rsyslog

# Docker Dockerfile best practices
cat > Dockerfile << 'EOF'
FROM node:20-alpine

# Create non-root user for application
RUN addgroup -g 1001 appgroup && \
    adduser -D -u 1001 -G appgroup appuser

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy application files
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .

# Install dependencies
RUN npm ci --only=production

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Use read-only root filesystem (mount writable volumes only where needed)
# Run container with: docker run --read-only -v /app/logs:/app/logs:rw myapp

EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
EOF

# Docker Compose with read-only root filesystem
cat > docker-compose.yml << 'EOF'
version: '3.8'
services:
  app:
    image: myapp:latest
    read_only: true
    user: "1001:1001"
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp
    volumes:
      # Only mount log directory as writable
      - app-logs:/app/logs:rw
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
    cap_drop:
      - ALL
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE

volumes:
  app-logs:
    driver: local
EOF

# Kubernetes Pod Security Context
cat > k8s-pod-security.yaml << 'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1001
    runAsGroup: 1001
    fsGroup: 1001
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:latest
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /app/logs
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
  volumes:
  - name: logs
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

iso27001_controls: A.9.4.5 - Access control to system and application functions
nist_csf: PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined and documented
PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
PR.AC-4 - Access permissions and authorizations are managed
validation_steps: Verify log files have 640 permissions: ls -la /var/log/
Confirm Docker containers run as non-root: docker inspect <container> | grep User
Test read-only root filesystem: docker run --read-only myapp
Attempt to modify logs as application user (should fail)
Review auditd rules for log file access monitoring

mitigations.title: Implement Real-Time Log Forwarding to Remote SIEM
mitigations.description: Forward all logs in real-time to a centralized SIEM or log management system where attackers cannot tamper with them. Use TLS encryption for log transmission and implement network segmentation to protect the logging infrastructure.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Configure rsyslog to forward logs to Azure Sentinel/Log Analytics

# Install rsyslog if not present
apt-get update && apt-get install -y rsyslog rsyslog-gnutls

# Create Azure Log Analytics configuration
cat > /etc/rsyslog.d/50-azure-sentinel.conf << 'EOF'
# Load required modules
module(load="omfwd")
module(load="imfile")

# Configure TLS for secure log transmission
global(
    DefaultNetstreamDriver="gtls"
    DefaultNetstreamDriverCAFile="/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt"
)

# Forward all logs to Azure Log Analytics
# Replace with your workspace ID and collector endpoint
*.* action(
    type="omfwd"
    target="<workspace-id>.ods.opinsights.azure.com"
    port="6514"
    protocol="tcp"
    StreamDriver="gtls"
    StreamDriverMode="1"
    StreamDriverAuthMode="x509/name"
    StreamDriverPermittedPeers="*.ods.opinsights.azure.com"
    queue.type="LinkedList"
    queue.size="10000"
    queue.dequeueBatchSize="100"
    queue.saveOnShutdown="on"
    action.resumeRetryCount="-1"
)

# Monitor custom application logs
input(type="imfile"
      File="/var/log/app/application.log"
      Tag="app"
      Severity="info"
      Facility="local0")

input(type="imfile"
      File="/var/log/app/error.log"
      Tag="app-error"
      Severity="error"
      Facility="local0")
EOF

# Set secure permissions on rsyslog config
chmod 640 /etc/rsyslog.d/50-azure-sentinel.conf
chown root:root /etc/rsyslog.d/50-azure-sentinel.conf

# Restart rsyslog
systemctl restart rsyslog
systemctl enable rsyslog

# Configure Docker to forward logs to syslog
cat > /etc/docker/daemon.json << 'EOF'
{
  "log-driver": "syslog",
  "log-opts": {
    "syslog-address": "tcp://127.0.0.1:514",
    "syslog-facility": "daemon",
    "tag": "{{.Name}}/{{.ID}}"
  }
}
EOF

systemctl restart docker

# Example Docker Compose with syslog logging
cat > docker-compose-syslog.yml << 'EOF'
version: '3.8'
services:
  app:
    image: myapp:latest
    logging:
      driver: syslog
      options:
        syslog-address: "tcp://syslog-server:514"
        syslog-facility: "daemon"
        tag: "{{.Name}}/{{.ID}}"
        syslog-format: "rfc5424"
EOF

# Alternative: Fluentd configuration for multi-destination forwarding
cat > /etc/fluentd/fluent.conf << 'EOF'
# Input from Docker containers
<source>
  @type forward
  port 24224
  bind 0.0.0.0
</source>

# Input from syslog
<source>
  @type syslog
  port 514
  bind 0.0.0.0
  tag system.syslog
</source>

# Forward to Azure Sentinel
<match **>
  @type azure-loganalytics
  workspace_id YOUR_WORKSPACE_ID
  workspace_key YOUR_WORKSPACE_KEY
  log_type CustomLogs
  <buffer>
    @type memory
    flush_interval 10s
    retry_max_times 3
  </buffer>
</match>

# Backup to S3 for long-term retention
<match **>
  @type copy
  <store>
    @type s3
    aws_key_id YOUR_AWS_KEY
    aws_sec_key YOUR_AWS_SECRET
    s3_bucket security-logs-backup
    s3_region us-east-1
    path logs/%Y/%m/%d/
    <buffer>
      @type file
      path /var/log/fluentd/s3
      timekey 3600
      timekey_wait 10m
    </buffer>
  </store>
</match>
EOF

nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
validation_steps: Verify rsyslog forwarding: logger -t test 'Test message' && check SIEM
Confirm Docker logs forwarding: docker logs <container> (should show forwarding)
Monitor rsyslog queue: rsyslogd -N1 to check configuration
Verify no local log tampering affects SIEM data

mitigations.title: Deploy Immutable Logging with WORM Storage and File Integrity Monitoring
mitigations.description: Implement write-once-read-many (WORM) storage for logs using Linux chattr +a (append-only) attribute or dedicated immutable storage solutions. Combine with file integrity monitoring (FIM) using tools like AIDE or Wazuh to detect any tampering attempts.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Implement immutable logging with append-only files and AIDE monitoring

# Step 1: Configure append-only attribute for critical logs
# This prevents deletion or modification, only allows appending

# Make logs append-only (even root cannot delete/modify)
chattr +a /var/log/auth.log
chattr +a /var/log/syslog
chattr +a /var/log/audit/audit.log
chattr +a /var/log/app/application.log

# Verify append-only attribute
lsattr /var/log/auth.log
# Output should show: -----a--------e----- /var/log/auth.log

# To remove append-only (requires reboot in secure systems):
# chattr -a /var/log/auth.log

# Step 2: Install and configure AIDE (Advanced Intrusion Detection Environment)
apt-get update && apt-get install -y aide aide-common

# Configure AIDE to monitor log files
cat >> /etc/aide/aide.conf << 'EOF'
# Monitor log directories for tampering
/var/log p+i+n+u+g+s+b+m+c+md5+sha256
/var/log/app p+i+n+u+g+s+b+m+c+md5+sha256
/var/log/audit p+i+n+u+g+s+b+m+c+md5+sha256

# Monitor rsyslog configuration
/etc/rsyslog.conf p+i+n+u+g+s+b+m+c+md5+sha256
/etc/rsyslog.d p+i+n+u+g+s+b+m+c+md5+sha256

# Exclude temporary and cache files
!/var/log/.*\.gz$
!/var/log/.*\.[0-9]$
EOF

# Initialize AIDE database (baseline)
aideinit
mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new /var/lib/aide/aide.db

# Run AIDE check (compare current state to baseline)
aide --check

# Schedule daily AIDE checks
cat > /etc/cron.daily/aide-check << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# Run AIDE integrity check and email results

LOG_FILE="/var/log/aide/aide-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).log"
mkdir -p /var/log/aide

aide --check > "$LOG_FILE" 2>&1

if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    # Changes detected - alert security team
    ALERT_MSG="AIDE detected file integrity violations on $(hostname)"
    echo "$ALERT_MSG" | mail -s "SECURITY ALERT: Log Tampering Detected" security@company.com
    
    # Send to SIEM
    logger -p local0.crit -t aide "$ALERT_MSG - See $LOG_FILE"
fi

# Keep last 30 days of AIDE logs
find /var/log/aide -name "aide-*.log" -mtime +30 -delete
EOF

chmod +x /etc/cron.daily/aide-check

# Step 3: Configure auditd rules for log file access monitoring
cat >> /etc/audit/rules.d/log-tampering.rules << 'EOF'
# Monitor write/delete attempts on log files
-w /var/log/ -p wa -k log_tampering
-w /var/log/auth.log -p wa -k auth_log_tampering
-w /var/log/audit/ -p wa -k audit_log_tampering
-w /var/log/app/ -p wa -k app_log_tampering

# Monitor chattr commands (used to remove append-only attribute)
-w /usr/bin/chattr -p x -k chattr_execution

# Monitor rsyslog configuration changes
-w /etc/rsyslog.conf -p wa -k rsyslog_config_change
-w /etc/rsyslog.d/ -p wa -k rsyslog_config_change
EOF

# Reload auditd rules
augenrules --load

# Step 4: Setup Wazuh agent for centralized FIM (alternative/complementary to AIDE)
cat > /var/ossec/etc/ossec.conf << 'EOF'
<ossec_config>
  <syscheck>
    <frequency>300</frequency>
    
    <!-- Monitor log files -->
    <directories check_all="yes" realtime="yes" report_changes="yes">
      /var/log/auth.log
    </directories>
    <directories check_all="yes" realtime="yes" report_changes="yes">
      /var/log/app/
    </directories>
    <directories check_all="yes" realtime="yes" report_changes="yes">
      /var/log/audit/
    </directories>
    
    <!-- Alert on file modification -->
    <alert_new_files>yes</alert_new_files>
    <auto_ignore>no</auto_ignore>
  </syscheck>
  
  <localfile>
    <log_format>syslog</log_format>
    <location>/var/log/auth.log</location>
  </localfile>
</ossec_config>
EOF

# Step 5: AWS S3 with Object Lock for immutable log storage (cloud option)
cat > upload-to-s3-worm.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# Upload logs to S3 bucket with Object Lock (WORM compliance mode)

BUCKET="compliance-logs-immutable"
LOG_FILE="/var/log/app/application.log"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y/%m/%d/%H%M%S)

# Upload to S3 with Object Lock retention
aws s3 cp "$LOG_FILE" \
    "s3://$BUCKET/logs/$TIMESTAMP/application.log" \
    --sse AES256 \
    --object-lock-mode COMPLIANCE \
    --object-lock-retain-until-date "$(date -d '+7 years' -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"

echo "Log uploaded to immutable S3 storage: s3://$BUCKET/logs/$TIMESTAMP/"
EOF

chmod +x upload-to-s3-worm.sh

# Schedule hourly uploads to WORM storage
echo "0 * * * * /usr/local/bin/upload-to-s3-worm.sh" | crontab -

echo "Immutable logging configured successfully:"
echo "- Append-only attribute set on critical logs"
echo "- AIDE monitoring initialized"
echo "- auditd rules configured for log access monitoring"
echo "- Optional: S3 Object Lock for cloud WORM storage"

nist_csf: PR.DS-6 - Integrity checking mechanisms are used to verify software, firmware, and information integrity
PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined, documented, implemented, and reviewed
PR.DS-8 - Integrity checking mechanisms are used to verify hardware integrity
validation_steps: Test append-only attribute: attempt to modify/delete log (should fail)
Run AIDE check: aide --check (should show no violations initially)
Simulate tampering: attempt to edit log and verify AIDE detection
Verify S3 Object Lock: attempt to delete uploaded log (should fail)

indicators: Gaps in log timestamps indicating deleted entries
File modification times (mtime) newer than log entry timestamps
Log file sizes decreasing unexpectedly (deletion of entries)
Sudden increase in disk space despite active logging
Missing authentication events during known user sessions
Log entries with timestamps in the future or past
Failed auditd or AIDE integrity checks

log_queries.query:
// Detect timestamp gaps in authentication logs suggesting log deletion
let TimeWindow = 1h;
SecurityEvent
| where EventID in (4624, 4625) // Successful/Failed logons
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| order by TimeGenerated asc
| extend PreviousTime = prev(TimeGenerated, 1)
| extend TimeDelta = datetime_diff('minute', TimeGenerated, PreviousTime)
| where TimeDelta > 60 // Gap larger than 60 minutes
| project TimeGenerated, Account, Computer, EventID, TimeDelta, PreviousTime
| summarize GapCount=count(), MaxGap=max(TimeDelta) by Computer, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h)
| where GapCount > 3 or MaxGap > 180

// Detect file modifications to log files
event.category: "file" AND event.action: ("modified" OR "deleted" OR "renamed") AND file.path: /var/log/* AND NOT user.name: ("syslog" OR "root")

index=linux sourcetype=linux_audit type=SYSCALL
| search (exe="/usr/bin/vi" OR exe="/usr/bin/nano" OR exe="/bin/sed" OR exe="/bin/rm") AND (name="/var/log/*.log" OR name="/var/log/audit/*")
| eval suspicious_activity="Log file modification detected"
| table _time, host, user, exe, name, key
| where key="log_tampering"

// Detect attempts to remove append-only attribute from logs
Syslog
| where Facility == "authpriv" or Facility == "audit"
| where SyslogMessage contains "chattr" and SyslogMessage contains "-a"
| where SyslogMessage contains "/var/log"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SyslogMessage, ProcessName
| extend AlertSeverity = "High"
| extend ThreatIndicator = "Attempt to make logs writable"

fields @timestamp, @message, @logStream
| filter @message like /ERROR|WARN/ or @message like /chattr|chmod.*\/var\/log/
| stats count() by bin(5m) as time_bucket
| sort time_bucket desc

tools.name: AIDE
tools.usage: aide --check # Compare current file state to baseline

tools.name: auditd
tools.usage: ausearch -k log_tampering -ts recent # Search for log tampering events

tools.name: Wazuh
tools.usage: Real-time FIM alerts on log file modifications

tools.name: Logstash
tools.usage: Detect timestamp anomalies and gaps in log sequences

compliance: ISO27001 (A.12.4.1, A.12.4.2 - Event logging and log protection)
